text:
This review provides a comprehensive overview of in situ ultraviolet-visible-near infrared (UV-VIS-NIR) absorption spectroscopy in the complex landscapes of thermal, electro- and photocatalysis. By exploiting electronic transitions, the technique offers invaluable information about the active species of the catalytic system including structural information, electronic states and kinetics. Experimental approaches and set-ups specifically designed for the investigation of catalytic systems in the time domain from femtoseconds to hours, such as stopped-flow techniques, laser-pulse transient absorption, and perturbation methods (such as SSITKA) are discussed.

This volume highlights the applicability of UV-VIS-NIR spectroscopy in identifying structural changes in catalysts during catalytic reactions such as identifying changes in oxidation states, identification of site markers in metal-exchanged zeolites and metalloenzymes and identification of chromophoric coke intermediates in acid catalysis. In addition, this volume emphasizes the power of working in combination of absorption spectroscopy with other spectroscopic techniques like magnetic circular dichroism (MCD) and resonance Raman (rR) spectroscopy to elucidate the coordination chemistry of transition metal centers in their catalytically active states. The volume also focuses on data processing and analysis, particularly for diffuse reflectance (DRS) spectra that require Kubelka-Munk transformation and for Tauc plots to derive the bandgap energy. Recent literature examples from both homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis have been compiled in this review to illustrate the applicability of spectrophotometry in bridging the relationship between electronic structures of catalysts and their catalytic activities.
